question:
6. Answer either (i) or (ii):
(i) Let \( V_{1}, V_{2}, V_{3} \), and \( V \) denote four vertices of a cube. \( V_{1}, V_{2} \), and \( V_{3} \) are next neighbors of \( V \), that is, the lines \( V V_{1}, V V_{2} \), and \( V V_{3} \) are edges of the cube. Project the cube orthogonally onto a plane (the \( z \)-plane, the Gaussian plane) of which the points are marked with complex numbers. Let the projection of \( V \) fall in the origin and the projections of \( V_{1}, V_{2} \), and \( V_{3} \) in points marked with the complex numbers \( z_{1}, z_{2} \), and \( z_{3} \), respectively. Show that \( z_{1}{ }^{2}+z_{2^{2}}+z_{3^{2}}=0 \).
(page 261)
(ii) Let \( a_{i j} \) be a determinant in which each diagonal element exceeds in absolute value the sum of the absolute values of the other elements of its row, that is
\[
\left|a_{i i}\right|>\left|a_{i 1}\right|+\left|a_{i 2}\right|+\cdots+\left|a_{i, i-1}\right|+\left|a_{i, i+1}\right|+\cdots+\left|a_{i n}\right|
\]

Show that the determinant is not equal to zero. (Consider the corresponding system of linear homogeneous equations.)
solution:
Solution. Take Cartesian coordinates \( w, x, y \) in space so that the \( x \)-axis is the real axis and the \( y \)-axis is the imaginary axis in the given Gaussian plane. Then the projection maps each point ( \( w, x, y \) ) onto \( (0, x, y) \).

Suppose ( \( w_{1}, x_{1}, y_{1} \) ), \( \left(w_{2}, x_{2}, y_{2}\right) \) and ( \( \left.w_{3}, x_{3}, y_{3}\right) \) are mutually orthogonal unit vectors in space. Then the matrix
\[
\left(\begin{array}{lll}
w_{1} & x_{1} & y_{1} \\
w_{2} & x_{2} & y_{2} \\
w_{3} & x_{3} & y_{3}
\end{array}\right)
\]
is an orthogonal matrix, so its columns are also mutually orthogonal unit vectors, in particular
\[
\begin{array}{c}
x_{1}^{2}+x_{2}^{2}+x_{3}^{2}=1 \\
y_{1}^{2}+y_{2}^{2}+y_{3}^{2}=1 \\
x_{1} y_{1}+x_{2} y_{2}+x_{3} y_{3}=0 .
\end{array}
\]

The orthogonal projections of the unit vectors into the Gaussian plane are the complex numbers \( x_{1}+i y_{1}, x_{2}+i y_{2} \), and \( x_{3}+i y_{3} \), and
\[
\begin{array}{c}
\left(x_{1}+i y_{1}\right)^{2}+\left(x_{2}+i y_{2}\right)^{2}+\left(x_{3}+i y_{3}\right)^{2}=x_{1}^{2}+x_{2}^{2}+x_{3}^{2}-y_{1}^{2} \\
-y_{2}^{2}-y_{3}^{2}+2 i\left(x_{1} y_{1}+x_{2} y_{2}+x_{3} y_{3}\right)=0 .
\end{array}
\]

Suppose the sides of the given cube are of length \( a \). Since it is given that the vertex \( V \) projects onto the origin, it must be of the form \( V=(b, 0,0) \). There are mutually orthogonal unit vectors ( \( w_{j}, x_{j}, y_{j} \) ) such that
\[
V_{j}=(b, 0,0)+a\left(w_{j}, x_{j}, y_{j}\right) \quad \text { for } j=1,2,3
\]

Then the projection of \( V_{j} \) into the Gaussian plane is
\[
z_{j}=a\left(x_{j}+i y_{j}\right)
\]
and
\[
z_{1}^{2}+z_{2}^{2}+z_{3}^{2}=a^{2}\left[\left(x_{1}+i y_{1}\right)^{2}+\left(x_{2}+i y_{2}\right)^{2}+\left(x_{3}+i y_{3}\right)^{2}\right]=0 .
\]

Solution. The corresponding system of linear equations is
\[
\sum_{j=1}^{n} a_{i j} x_{j}=0, \quad i=1,2, \ldots, n
\]

If the determinant of the matrix of the coefficients is zero, there exists a non-trivial solution, say ( \( \bar{x}_{1}, \bar{x}_{2}, \ldots, \bar{x}_{n} \) ), of the system. Let \( m \) be an index for which \( \left|\bar{x}_{m}\right| \) is largest, that is \( \left|\bar{x}_{m}\right| \geq\left|\bar{x}_{j}\right| \) for \( j=1,2, \ldots, n \). Clearly, \( \left|\bar{x}_{m}\right| \neq 0 \), since the solution is non-trivial. Consider the \( m \) th equation in the above system written in the form
\[
-a_{m m} \bar{x}_{m}=\sum_{j \neq m} a_{m j} \bar{x}_{j}
\]

We have
\[
\left|a_{m m}\right|\left|\bar{x}_{m}\right| \leq \sum_{j \neq m}\left|a_{m j}\right|\left|\bar{x}_{j}\right| \leq\left(\sum_{j \neq m}\left|a_{m j}\right|\right)\left|\bar{x}_{m}\right|
\]
and therefore
\[
\left|a_{m m}\right| \leq \sum_{j \neq m}\left|a_{m j}\right|
\]
contrary to hypothesis. Hence the determinant cannot be zero.
Remark. This is the same type of argument that was used by Gersgorin to obtain bounds on the eigenvalues of a matrix. See Marcus and Minc, A Survey of Matrix Theory and Matrix Inequalities, Allyn and Bacon, Boston, 1964, page 146.

descriptive_long.question:
6. Answer either (i) or (ii):
(i) Let \( cubevone, cubevtwo, cubevthree \), and \( cubevert \) denote four vertices of a cube. \( cubevone, cubevtwo \), and \( cubevthree \) are next neighbors of \( cubevert \), that is, the lines \( cubevert cubevone, cubevert cubevtwo \), and \( cubevert cubevthree \) are edges of the cube. Project the cube orthogonally onto a plane (the \( complexz \)-plane, the Gaussian plane) of which the points are marked with complex numbers. Let the projection of \( cubevert \) fall in the origin and the projections of \( cubevone, cubevtwo \), and \( cubevthree \) in points marked with the complex numbers \( complexzone, complexztwo \), and \( complexzthree \), respectively. Show that \( complexzone^{2}+complexztwo^{2}+complexzthree^{2}=0 \).
(page 261)
(ii) Let \( genentry \) be a determinant in which each diagonal element exceeds in absolute value the sum of the absolute values of the other elements of its row, that is
\[
\left|diagentry\right|>\left|entryfirst\right|+\left|entrysecond\right|+\cdots+\left|genentry_{i, i-1}\right|+\left|genentry_{i, i+1}\right|+\cdots+\left|entrylast\right|
\]

Show that the determinant is not equal to zero. (Consider the corresponding system of linear homogeneous equations.)
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. Take Cartesian coordinates \( coordw, coordx, coordy \) in space so that the \( coordx \)-axis is the real axis and the \( coordy \)-axis is the imaginary axis in the given Gaussian plane. Then the projection maps each point \( ( coordw, coordx, coordy ) \) onto \( (0, coordx, coordy) \).

Suppose \( ( vectorwone, vectorxone, vectoryone ) , ( vectorwtwo, vectorxtwo, vectorytwo ) \) and \( ( vectorwthree, vectorxthree, vectorythree ) \) are mutually orthogonal unit vectors in space. Then the matrix
\[
\left(\begin{array}{lll}
vectorwone & vectorxone & vectoryone \\
vectorwtwo & vectorxtwo & vectorytwo \\
vectorwthree & vectorxthree & vectorythree
\end{array}\right)
\]
is an orthogonal matrix, so its columns are also mutually orthogonal unit vectors, in particular
\[
\begin{array}{c}
vectorxone^{2}+vectorxtwo^{2}+vectorxthree^{2}=1 \\
vectoryone^{2}+vectorytwo^{2}+vectorythree^{2}=1 \\
vectorxone\,vectoryone+vectorxtwo\,vectorytwo+vectorxthree\,vectorythree=0 .
\end{array}
\]

The orthogonal projections of the unit vectors into the Gaussian plane are the complex numbers \( vectorxone+i\,vectoryone, vectorxtwo+i\,vectorytwo \), and \( vectorxthree+i\,vectorythree \), and
\[
\begin{array}{c}
\left(vectorxone+i\,vectoryone\right)^{2}+\left(vectorxtwo+i\,vectorytwo\right)^{2}+\left(vectorxthree+i\,vectorythree\right)^{2}=vectorxone^{2}+vectorxtwo^{2}+vectorxthree^{2}-vectoryone^{2} \\
-vectorytwo^{2}-vectorythree^{2}+2 i\left(vectorxone\,vectoryone+vectorxtwo\,vectorytwo+vectorxthree\,vectorythree\right)=0 .
\end{array}
\]

Suppose the sides of the given cube are of length \( sidelength \). Since it is given that the vertex \( cubevert \) projects onto the origin, it must be of the form \( cubevert=(offsetval, 0,0) \). There are mutually orthogonal unit vectors \( ( vectorwgen, vectorxgen, vectorygen ) \) such that
\[
cubevone=(offsetval, 0,0)+sidelength( vectorwone, vectorxone, vectoryone ),\quad
cubevtwo=(offsetval, 0,0)+sidelength( vectorwtwo, vectorxtwo, vectorytwo ),\quad
cubevthree=(offsetval, 0,0)+sidelength( vectorwthree, vectorxthree, vectorythree )
\]

Then the projection of \( cubevone, cubevtwo, cubevthree \) into the Gaussian plane is
\[
complexzone=sidelength\left(vectorxone+i\,vectoryone\right),\quad
complexztwo=sidelength\left(vectorxtwo+i\,vectorytwo\right),\quad
complexzthree=sidelength\left(vectorxthree+i\,vectorythree\right)
\]
and
\[
complexzone^{2}+complexztwo^{2}+complexzthree^{2}=sidelength^{2}\left[\left(vectorxone+i\,vectoryone\right)^{2}+\left(vectorxtwo+i\,vectorytwo\right)^{2}+\left(vectorxthree+i\,vectorythree\right)^{2}\right]=0 .
\]

Solution. The corresponding system of linear equations is
\[
\sum_{indexvar=1}^{dimsize} genentry\,vectorxgen =0, \quad i=1,2, \ldots, dimsize
\]

If the determinant of the matrix of the coefficients is zero, there exists a non-trivial solution, say \( ( \bar{vectorxone}, \bar{vectorxtwo}, \ldots, \bar{vectorxgen} ) \), of the system. Let \( maxindex \) be an index for which \( |\bar{vectorxmax}| \) is largest, that is \( |\bar{vectorxmax}| \ge |\bar{vectorxgen}| \) for \( indexvar=1,2, \ldots, dimsize \). Clearly, \( |\bar{vectorxmax}| \neq 0 \), since the solution is non-trivial. Consider the \( maxindex \) th equation in the above system written in the form
\[
-rowmdiag \, \bar{vectorxmax}=\sum_{indexvar \neq maxindex} rowmentry \, \bar{vectorxgen}
\]

We have
\[
|rowmdiag|\,|\bar{vectorxmax}| \le \sum_{indexvar \neq maxindex}|rowmentry|\,|\bar{vectorxgen}| \le \left(\sum_{indexvar \neq maxindex}|rowmentry|\right)|\bar{vectorxmax}|
\]
and therefore
\[
|rowmdiag| \le \sum_{indexvar \neq maxindex}|rowmentry|
\]
contrary to hypothesis. Hence the determinant cannot be zero.
Remark. This is the same type of argument that was used by Gersgorin to obtain bounds on the eigenvalues of a matrix. See Marcus and Minc, A Survey of Matrix Theory and Matrix Inequalities, Allyn and Bacon, Boston, 1964, page 146.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
6. Answer either (i) or (ii):
(i) Let \( thistledrift, silverspoon, marigoldpetal \), and \( cobblestone \) denote four vertices of a cube. \( thistledrift, silverspoon \), and \( marigoldpetal \) are next neighbors of \( cobblestone \), that is, the lines \( cobblestone thistledrift, cobblestone silverspoon \), and \( cobblestone marigoldpetal \) are edges of the cube. Project the cube orthogonally onto a plane (the \( primrosegale \)-plane, the Gaussian plane) of which the points are marked with complex numbers. Let the projection of \( cobblestone \) fall in the origin and the projections of \( thistledrift, silverspoon \), and \( marigoldpetal \) in points marked with the complex numbers \( opalhollow, jasperdawn \), and \( onyxrefuge \), respectively. Show that \( opalhollow^{2}+jasperdawn^{2}+onyxrefuge^{2}=0 \).
(page 261)
(ii) Let \( willowbranch \) be a determinant in which each diagonal element exceeds in absolute value the sum of the absolute values of the other elements of its row, that is
\[
\left|ravenquartz\right|>\left|porcelainjar\right|+\left|meadowlark\right|+\cdots+\left|twilightdew\right|
\]
Show that the determinant is not equal to zero. (Consider the corresponding system of linear homogeneous equations.)
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. Take Cartesian coordinates \( glaciermist, elmrootvine, rosequartz \) in space so that the \( elmrootvine \)-axis is the real axis and the \( rosequartz \)-axis is the imaginary axis in the given Gaussian plane. Then the projection maps each point ( \( glaciermist, elmrootvine, rosequartz \) ) onto \( (0, elmrootvine, rosequartz) \).

Suppose ( \( cranberryfog, sagehorizon, lilacbreeze \) ), \( (sunsetember, basilmeadow, peachripple) \) and ( \( ivorycandle, cedarwhisper, cobaltstream \) ) are mutually orthogonal unit vectors in space. Then the matrix
\[
\left(\begin{array}{lll}
cranberryfog & sagehorizon & lilacbreeze \\
sunsetember & basilmeadow & peachripple \\
ivorycandle & cedarwhisper & cobaltstream
\end{array}\right)
\]
is an orthogonal matrix, so its columns are also mutually orthogonal unit vectors, in particular
\[
\begin{array}{c}
sagehorizon^{2}+basilmeadow^{2}+cedarwhisper^{2}=1 \\
lilacbreeze^{2}+peachripple^{2}+cobaltstream^{2}=1 \\
sagehorizon\,lilacbreeze+basilmeadow\,peachripple+cedarwhisper\,cobaltstream=0 .
\end{array}
\]

The orthogonal projections of the unit vectors into the Gaussian plane are the complex numbers \( sagehorizon+i\,lilacbreeze, basilmeadow+i\,peachripple \), and \( cedarwhisper+i\,cobaltstream \), and
\[
\begin{array}{c}
\left(sagehorizon+i\,lilacbreeze\right)^{2}+\left(basilmeadow+i\,peachripple\right)^{2}+\left(cedarwhisper+i\,cobaltstream\right)^{2}=sagehorizon^{2}+basilmeadow^{2}+cedarwhisper^{2}-lilacbreeze^{2}\\
-peachripple^{2}-cobaltstream^{2}+2 i\left(sagehorizon\,lilacbreeze+basilmeadow\,peachripple+cedarwhisper\,cobaltstream\right)=0 .
\end{array}
\]

Suppose the sides of the given cube are of length \( lindenbranch \). Since it is given that the vertex \( cobblestone \) projects onto the origin, it must be of the form \( cobblestone=(aldercanyon,0,0) \). There are mutually orthogonal unit vectors ( \( glaciermist_{\woodpecker}, elmrootvine_{\woodpecker}, rosequartz_{\woodpecker} \) ) such that
\[
thistledrift=(aldercanyon,0,0)+lindenbranch\,(cranberryfog, sagehorizon, lilacbreeze),\\
silverspoon=(aldercanyon,0,0)+lindenbranch\,(sunsetember, basilmeadow, peachripple),\\
marigoldpetal=(aldercanyon,0,0)+lindenbranch\,(ivorycandle, cedarwhisper, cobaltstream)
\]

Then the projection of \( thistledrift, silverspoon, marigoldpetal \) into the Gaussian plane is
\[
opalhollow=lindenbranch\,(sagehorizon+i\,lilacbreeze),\quad
jasperdawn=lindenbranch\,(basilmeadow+i\,peachripple),\quad
onyxrefuge=lindenbranch\,(cedarwhisper+i\,cobaltstream)
\]
and
\[
opalhollow^{2}+jasperdawn^{2}+onyxrefuge^{2}=lindenbranch^{2}\bigl[\left(sagehorizon+i\,lilacbreeze\right)^{2}+\left(basilmeadow+i\,peachripple\right)^{2}+\left(cedarwhisper+i\,cobaltstream\right)^{2}\bigr]=0 .
\]

Solution. The corresponding system of linear equations is
\[
\sum_{woodpecker=1}^{galaxyorbit} willowbranch\,x_{woodpecker}=0,\quad i=1,2,\ldots,galaxyorbit
\]
If the determinant of the matrix of the coefficients is zero, there exists a non-trivial solution, say ( \( \bar{x}_{1}, \bar{x}_{2}, \ldots, \bar{x}_{galaxyorbit} \) ), of the system. Let \( riverstone \) be an index for which \( |\bar{x}_{riverstone}| \) is largest, that is \( |\bar{x}_{riverstone}| \ge |\bar{x}_{j}| \) for \( j=1,2,\ldots,galaxyorbit \). Clearly, \( |\bar{x}_{riverstone}| \neq 0 \), since the solution is non-trivial. Consider the \( riverstone \)th equation in the above system written in the form
\[
-fjordlantern\,\bar{x}_{riverstone}=\sum_{woodpecker \neq riverstone} hazelnutseed\,\bar{x}_{woodpecker}
\]
We have
\[
|fjordlantern|\,|\bar{x}_{riverstone}| \le \sum_{woodpecker \neq riverstone}|hazelnutseed|\,|\bar{x}_{woodpecker}| \le \left(\sum_{woodpecker \neq riverstone}|hazelnutseed|\right)|\bar{x}_{riverstone}|
\]
and therefore
\[
|fjordlantern| \le \sum_{woodpecker \neq riverstone}|hazelnutseed|
\]
contrary to hypothesis. Hence the determinant cannot be zero.
Remark. This is the same type of argument that was used by Gersgorin to obtain bounds on the eigenvalues of a matrix. See Marcus and Minc, A Survey of Matrix Theory and Matrix Inequalities, Allyn and Bacon, Boston, 1964, page 146.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
6. Answer either (i) or (ii):
(i) Let \( farvertexone, farvertextwo, farvertexthr \), and \( centerpoint \) denote four vertices of a cube. \( farvertexone, farvertextwo \), and \( farvertexthr \) are next neighbors of \( centerpoint \), that is, the lines \( centerpoint farvertexone, centerpoint farvertextwo \), and \( centerpoint farvertexthr \) are edges of the cube. Project the cube orthogonally onto a plane (the \( realnumber\)-plane, the Gaussian plane) of which the points are marked with complex numbers. Let the projection of \( centerpoint \) fall in the origin and the projections of \( farvertexone, farvertextwo \), and \( farvertexthr \) in points marked with the complex numbers \( realnumberone, realnumbertwo \), and \( realnumberthr \), respectively. Show that \( realnumberone { }^{2}+realnumbertwo^{2}+realnumberthr^{2}=0 \).
(page 261)
(ii) Let \( zeroelement \) be a determinant in which each diagonal element exceeds in absolute value the sum of the absolute values of the other elements of its row, that is
\[
\left|offdiagonal\right|>\left|lastcolumn\right|+\left|randomcolumn\right|+\cdots+\left|a_{i, i-1}\right|+\left|a_{i, i+1}\right|+\cdots+\left|firstcolumn\right|
\]

Show that the determinant is not equal to zero. (Consider the corresponding system of linear homogeneous equations.)
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. Take Cartesian coordinates \( flatcoord, imagaxis, realaxis \) in space so that the \( imagaxis \)-axis is the real axis and the \( realaxis \)-axis is the imaginary axis in the given Gaussian plane. Then the projection maps each point ( \( flatcoord, imagaxis, realaxis \) ) onto \( (0, imagaxis, realaxis) \).

Suppose ( \( flatcoordone, imagaxisone, realaxisone \) ), \( \left(flatcoordtwo, imagaxistwo, realaxistwo\right) \) and ( \( \left.flatcoordthr, imagaxisthr, realaxisthr\right) \) are mutually orthogonal unit vectors in space. Then the matrix
\[
\left(\begin{array}{lll}
flatcoordone & imagaxisone & realaxisone \\
flatcoordtwo & imagaxistwo & realaxistwo \\
flatcoordthr & imagaxisthr & realaxisthr
\end{array}\right)
\]
is an orthogonal matrix, so its columns are also mutually orthogonal unit vectors, in particular
\[
\begin{array}{c}
imagaxisone^{2}+imagaxistwo^{2}+imagaxisthr^{2}=1 \\
realaxisone^{2}+realaxistwo^{2}+realaxisthr^{2}=1 \\
imagaxisone\,realaxisone+imagaxistwo\,realaxistwo+imagaxisthr\,realaxisthr=0 .
\end{array}
\]

The orthogonal projections of the unit vectors into the Gaussian plane are the complex numbers \( imagaxisone+i\,realaxisone, imagaxistwo+i\,realaxistwo \), and \( imagaxisthr+i\,realaxisthr \), and
\[
\begin{array}{c}
\left(imagaxisone+i\,realaxisone\right)^{2}+\left(imagaxistwo+i\,realaxistwo\right)^{2}+\left(imagaxisthr+i\,realaxisthr\right)^{2}=imagaxisone^{2}+imagaxistwo^{2}+imagaxisthr^{2}-realaxisone^{2} \\
-realaxistwo^{2}-realaxisthr^{2}+2 i\left(imagaxisone\,realaxisone+imagaxistwo\,realaxistwo+imagaxisthr\,realaxisthr\right)=0 .
\end{array}
\]

Suppose the sides of the given cube are of length \( zeroedge \). Since it is given that the vertex \( centerpoint \) projects onto the origin, it must be of the form \( centerpoint=(zeroshift, 0,0) \). There are mutually orthogonal unit vectors ( \( flatcoordvar, imagaxisvar, realaxisvar \) ) such that
\[
V_{j}=(zeroshift, 0,0)+zeroedge\left(flatcoordvar, imagaxisvar, realaxisvar\right) \quad \text { for } j=1,2,3
\]

Then the projection of \( V_{j} \) into the Gaussian plane is
\[
realnumber_{j}=zeroedge\left(imagaxisvar+i\,realaxisvar\right)
\]
and
\[
realnumberone^{2}+realnumbertwo^{2}+realnumberthr^{2}=zeroedge^{2}\left[\left(imagaxisone+i\,realaxisone\right)^{2}+\left(imagaxistwo+i\,realaxistwo\right)^{2}+\left(imagaxisthr+i\,realaxisthr\right)^{2}\right]=0 .
\]

Solution. The corresponding system of linear equations is
\[
\sum_{constantindex=1}^{unitysize} zeroelement\,imagaxisvar=0, \quad constantindex=1,2, \ldots, unitysize
\]

If the determinant of the matrix of the coefficients is zero, there exists a non-trivial solution, say ( \( \bar{imagaxisone}, \bar{imagaxistwo}, \ldots, \bar{imagaxismin} \) ), of the system. Let \( minimumindex \) be an index for which \( \left|\bar{imagaxismin}\right| \) is largest, that is \( \left|\bar{imagaxismin}\right| \geq\left|\bar{imagaxisvar}\right| \) for \( constantindex=1,2, \ldots, unitysize \). Clearly, \( \left|\bar{imagaxismin}\right| \neq 0 \), since the solution is non-trivial. Consider the \( minimumindex \) th equation in the above system written in the form
\[
-outercorner \bar{imagaxismin}=\sum_{constantindex \neq minimumindex} fixedvalue \bar{imagaxisvar}
\]

We have
\[
\left|outercorner\right|\left|\bar{imagaxismin}\right| \leq \sum_{constantindex \neq minimumindex}\left|fixedvalue\right|\left|\bar{imagaxisvar}\right| \leq\left(\sum_{constantindex \neq minimumindex}\left|fixedvalue\right|\right)\left|\bar{imagaxismin}\right|
\]
and therefore
\[
\left|outercorner\right| \leq \sum_{constantindex \neq minimumindex}\left|fixedvalue\right|
\]
contrary to hypothesis. Hence the determinant cannot be zero.
Remark. This is the same type of argument that was used by Gersgorin to obtain bounds on the eigenvalues of a matrix. See Marcus and Minc, A Survey of Matrix Theory and Matrix Inequalities, Allyn and Bacon, Boston, 1964, page 146.

garbled_string.question:
6. Answer either (i) or (ii):
(i) Let \( jrukspad, vexlopar, cmandoti \), and \( wibnexla \) denote four vertices of a cube. \( jrukspad, vexlopar \), and \( cmandoti \) are next neighbors of \( wibnexla \), that is, the lines \( wibnexla\, jrukspad, wibnexla\, vexlopar \), and \( wibnexla\, cmandoti \) are edges of the cube. Project the cube orthogonally onto a plane (the \( tzcrmepl \)-plane, the Gaussian plane) of which the points are marked with complex numbers. Let the projection of \( wibnexla \) fall in the origin and the projections of \( jrukspad, vexlopar \), and \( cmandoti \) in points marked with the complex numbers \( akpldros, qrwstnfa \), and \( zxmorbih \), respectively. Show that \( akpldros^{2}+qrwstnfa^{2}+zxmorbih^{2}=0 \).
(page 261)
(ii) Let \( hjgrksla \) be a determinant in which each diagonal element exceeds in absolute value the sum of the absolute values of the other elements of its row, that is
\[
\left|qzxwvtnp\right|>\left|fkdlsmnb\right|+\left|prtuvyqe\right|+\cdots+\left|a_{i, i-1}\right|+\left|a_{i, i+1}\right|+\cdots+\left|ceghbalu\right|
\]

Show that the determinant is not equal to zero. (Consider the corresponding system of linear homogeneous equations.)
garbled_string.solution:
Solution. Take Cartesian coordinates \( iqoskftr, pfarnedu, lsoravqe \) in space so that the \( pfarnedu \)-axis is the real axis and the \( lsoravqe \)-axis is the imaginary axis in the given Gaussian plane. Then the projection maps each point ( \( iqoskftr, pfarnedu, lsoravqe \) ) onto \( (0, pfarnedu, lsoravqe) \).

Suppose ( \( exlartqb, bzqustla, orpltune \) ), \( \left(fqunsdmi, gwelmokn, qsdafwom\right) \) and ( \( \left.gvmopzle, nycravop, jkarnote\right) \) are mutually orthogonal unit vectors in space. Then the matrix
\[
\left(\begin{array}{lll}
exlartqb & bzqustla & orpltune \\
fqunsdmi & gwelmokn & qsdafwom \\
gvmopzle & nycravop & jkarnote
\end{array}\right)
\]
is an orthogonal matrix, so its columns are also mutually orthogonal unit vectors, in particular
\[
\begin{array}{c}
bzqustla^{2}+gwelmokn^{2}+nycravop^{2}=1 \\
orpltune^{2}+qsdafwom^{2}+jkarnote^{2}=1 \\
bzqustla\, orpltune+gwelmokn\, qsdafwom+nycravop\, jkarnote=0 .
\end{array}
\]

The orthogonal projections of the unit vectors into the Gaussian plane are the complex numbers \( bzqustla+i\, orpltune, gwelmokn+i\, qsdafwom \), and \( nycravop+i\, jkarnote \), and
\[
\begin{array}{c}
\left(bzqustla+i\, orpltune\right)^{2}+\left(gwelmokn+i\, qsdafwom\right)^{2}+\left(nycravop+i\, jkarnote\right)^{2}=bzqustla^{2}+gwelmokn^{2}+nycravop^{2}-orpltune^{2} \\
-qsdafwom^{2}-jkarnote^{2}+2 i\left(bzqustla\, orpltune+gwelmokn\, qsdafwom+nycravop\, jkarnote\right)=0 .
\end{array}
\]

Suppose the sides of the given cube are of length \( mcfaluzo \). Since it is given that the vertex \( wibnexla \) projects onto the origin, it must be of the form \( wibnexla=(nygabrot, 0,0) \). There are mutually orthogonal unit vectors ( \( ksyqdrav, hmalrefo, vlonskme \) ) such that
\[
V_{j}=(nygabrot, 0,0)+mcfaluzo\left(ksyqdrav, hmalrefo, vlonskme\right) \quad \text { for } j=1,2,3
\]

Then the projection of \( V_{j} \) into the Gaussian plane is
\[
z_{j}=mcfaluzo\left(hmalrefo+i\, vlonskme\right)
\]
and
\[
akpldros^{2}+qrwstnfa^{2}+zxmorbih^{2}=mcfaluzo^{2}\left[\left(hmalrefo+i\, vlonskme\right)^{2}+\left(hmalrefo+i\, vlonskme\right)^{2}+\left(hmalrefo+i\, vlonskme\right)^{2}\right]=0 .
\]

Solution. The corresponding system of linear equations is
\[
\sum_{omlasfne=1}^{plidertas} hjgrksla\, hmalrefo=0, \quad i=1,2, \ldots, plidertas
\]

If the determinant of the matrix of the coefficients is zero, there exists a non-trivial solution, say ( \( \bar{bzqustla}, \bar{gwelmokn}, \ldots, \bar{dzikpuna} \) ), of the system. Let \( qlirepso \) be an index for which \( \left|\bar{dzikpuna}\right| \) is largest, that is \( \left|\bar{dzikpuna}\right| \geq\left|\bar{x}_{j}\right| \) for \( j=1,2, \ldots, plidertas \). Clearly, \( \left|\bar{dzikpuna}\right| \neq 0 \), since the solution is non-trivial. Consider the \( qlirepso \) th equation in the above system written in the form
\[
-yvclirps\, \bar{dzikpuna}=\sum_{j \neq qlirepso} guorfkzn\, \bar{x}_{j}
\]

We have
\[
\left|yvclirps\right|\left|\bar{dzikpuna}\right| \leq \sum_{j \neq qlirepso}\left|guorfkzn\right|\left|\bar{x}_{j}\right| \leq\left(\sum_{j \neq qlirepso}\left|guorfkzn\right|\right)\left|\bar{dzikpuna}\right|
\]
and therefore
\[
\left|yvclirps\right| \leq \sum_{j \neq qlirepso}\left|guorfkzn\right|
\]
contrary to hypothesis. Hence the determinant cannot be zero.
Remark. This is the same type of argument that was used by Gersgorin to obtain bounds on the eigenvalues of a matrix. See Marcus and Minc, A Survey of Matrix Theory and Matrix Inequalities, Allyn and Bacon, Boston, 1964, page 146.

kernel_variant.question:
(The statement of the enhanced problem is unchanged, because the error pointed out by the reviewers concerns only the derivation given in the solution of Part (A)(ii).  For convenience we reproduce Part (A) verbatim.)

Solve EITHER Part (A) or Part (B).

(A)  (Geometry in 3-space viewed through complex coordinates)  
Let W be a vertex of a cube in \mathbb{R}^3; write WW_1, WW_2, WW_3 for the three mutually
perpendicular edges that emanate from W and assume they are oriented so that
(WW_1,WW_2,WW_3) is a positively oriented orthonormal frame.  
Fix an arbitrary two-dimensional plane \Pi  through W and denote by \pi :\mathbb{R}^3\to \Pi  the
orthogonal projection.  
Choose an orthonormal positively oriented basis (e_1,e_2) of \Pi , declare e_3:=e_1\times e_2, and identify \Pi  with the complex plane \mathbb{C} via
   \xi e_1+\eta e_2  \mapsto   \xi +i\eta  .      
Put
   \zeta _k   =   \pi (W_k) - \pi (W)   \in  \mathbb{C},      k=1,2,3,      (1)
and write
   a_k:=|\zeta _k| ,  c_k:=e_3\cdot WW_k .               (2)
(Observe that the (common) edge length of the cube equals a:=\|WW_k\|, so that  
a_k = a\sqrt{1-c_k^2}.)

(i)  (Quadratic identity) Prove that
   \zeta _1^2 + \zeta _2^2 + \zeta _3^2 = 0.                 (3)

(ii)  (A sharp upper bound for the cubic product)  

  Establish the exact modulus identity  
   |\zeta _1 \zeta _2 \zeta _3| = a^3 \sqrt{ (1-c_1^2)(1-c_2^2)(1-c_3^2) }.    (4)

  Using the constraint  
   c_1^2 + c_2^2 + c_3^2 = 1                 (5)
  that follows from the orthonormality of the edges, deduce the sharp
  inequality
   |\zeta _1 \zeta _2 \zeta _3| \leq  a^3 (2/3)^{3/2}.              (6)

  Show that equality in (6) holds exactly when  
   |\zeta _1| = |\zeta _2| = |\zeta _3|  \Leftrightarrow   |c_1| = |c_2| = |c_3| = 1/\sqrt{3.}  (7)

(iii)  (Reconstruction problem) [statement identical to the current enhanced version]

(B)  (Block-operator Gershgorin type theorem - unchanged)  
[statement identical to the original enhanced version]

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
kernel_variant.solution:
Only Part (A)(ii) is altered.  Parts (i) and (iii) were already correct and
are repeated for completeness.

Solution to Part (A)

Throughout put a:=\|WW_k\|.  For k=1,2,3 write
  u_k := WW_k /a, so that (u_1,u_2,u_3) is a positively oriented
  orthonormal basis of \mathbb{R}^3.                (9)

Denote
  x_k = u_k\cdot e_1 ,  y_k = u_k\cdot e_2 ,  c_k = u_k\cdot e_3 .      (10)

Because the u_k form an orthonormal triple, the 3 \times  3 matrix with k-th column
u_k is in SO(3); hence its rows
  X := (x_1,x_2,x_3) ,  Y := (y_1,y_2,y_3) ,  C := (c_1,c_2,c_3) (11)
constitute an orthonormal positively oriented frame, i.e.
  \|X\| = \|Y\| = \|C\| = 1 ,  X\cdot Y = X\cdot C = Y\cdot C = 0.    (12)

Further, by (1) and (10)
  \zeta _k = a (x_k + i y_k) ,  a_k = a\sqrt{1-c_k^2}.       (13)

(i)  Quadratic identity (3).  
 \zeta _1^2+\zeta _2^2+\zeta _3^2
  = a^2 \Sigma  (x_k+iy_k)^2
  = a^2[ \Sigma (x_k^2-y_k^2) + 2i \Sigma  x_ky_k ]
  = a^2[(\|X\|^2-\|Y\|^2) + 2i (X\cdot Y)] = 0 by (12).

(ii)  Modulus formula (4) and sharp bound (6).

From (13)
 |\zeta _1 \zeta _2 \zeta _3|
  = a^3 |x_1+iy_1| |x_2+iy_2| |x_3+iy_3|
  = a^3 \sqrt{(x_1^2+y_1^2)(x_2^2+y_2^2)(x_3^2+y_3^2)}
  = a^3 \sqrt{(1-c_1^2)(1-c_2^2)(1-c_3^2)},  which is (4).

Introduce s_k := c_k^2 (so 0\leq s_k\leq 1 and s_1+s_2+s_3=1 by (12)).  Then (4) becomes
 |\zeta _1 \zeta _2 \zeta _3| = a^3 P(s_1,s_2,s_3)^{1/2},
where
 P(s_1,s_2,s_3):=(1-s_1)(1-s_2)(1-s_3).         (16)

We must maximise P under the linear constraint s_1+s_2+s_3=1.

Method 1: Lagrange multipliers  
Let F(s_1,s_2,s_3,\lambda )=ln P+\lambda (s_1+s_2+s_3-1).  Computing
 \partial F/\partial s_1 = -1/(1-s_1)+\lambda , etc.,
we obtain critical points only when 1-s_1=1-s_2=1-s_3, i.e. s_1=s_2=s_3=1/3.  
Because P(\cdot ) is symmetric and positive on the open simplex 0<s_k<1, the
unique critical point is the (global) maximiser.

Method 2: sharp AM-GM (shorter)  
By AM-GM,
 P = (1-s_1)(1-s_2)(1-s_3)
  \leq  [(1-s_1)+(1-s_2)+(1-s_3)]^3/27
  = (2/3)^3,                      (17')
with equality iff 1-s_1 = 1-s_2 = 1-s_3, i.e. s_1=s_2=s_3=1/3.

Either way,
 P_max = (2/3)^3, hence |\zeta _1 \zeta _2 \zeta _3|
  \leq  a^3 (2/3)^{3/2}.                 (6)

Equality holds precisely when s_1=s_2=s_3=1/3, i.e. |c_1|=|c_2|=|c_3|=1/\sqrt{3},
which by (13) is equivalent to |\zeta _1|=|\zeta _2|=|\zeta _3|.  This is (7).

(iii)  Reconstruction of the cube from prescribed complex projections.  
[Identical to the previous solution and reproduced here for completeness.]

Let z_1,z_2,z_3 satisfy (8) and define
 X := (Re z_1,Re z_2,Re z_3), Y := (Im z_1,Im z_2,Im z_3),
 L := \|X\| = \|Y\| (>0).               (18)

From (8) we have X\cdot Y = 0.  Put
 Z := (1/L) X\times Y.                  (19)

Since X\bot Y and |X\times Y| = L^2, the 3 \times  3 matrix
 R := 1/L \cdot 
    Re z_1 Re z_2 Re z_3 
    Im z_1 Im z_2 Im z_3 
     Z_1  Z_2  Z_3                 (20)
has orthonormal rows; det R>0 because the third row is the right-hand
cross-product of the first two.  Hence R\in SO(3).

Let its columns be u_1,u_2,u_3 and place W at the origin.  Setting a:=L and
W_k := a u_k (k=1,2,3) yields a cube whose edges are WW_1,WW_2,WW_3 and whose
projections are precisely z_1,z_2,z_3.

Uniqueness up to rigid motion.  
If another oriented cube projects to the same unordered set {z_1,z_2,z_3},
the first two rows X,Y of the associated matrix (20) must coincide with or
be obtained from the above by a common permutation; consequently Z and the
entire rotation matrix R agree, so the cubes differ only by translating W.

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Solution to Part (B)

[Unchanged.]

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

original_kernel_variant.solution:
Only Part (A)(ii) is altered.  Parts (i) and (iii) were already correct and
are repeated for completeness.

  u_k := WW_k /a, so that (u_1,u_2,u_3) is a positively oriented

Because the u_k form an orthonormal triple, the 3 \times  3 matrix with k-th column

  X := (x_1,x_2,x_3) ,  Y := (y_1,y_2,y_3) ,  C := (c_1,c_2,c_3) (11)
constitute an orthonormal positively oriented frame, i.e.
  \|X\| = \|Y\| = \|C\| = 1 ,  X\cdot Y = X\cdot C = Y\cdot C = 0.    (12)

Further, by (1) and (10)
  \zeta _k = a (x_k + i y_k) ,  a_k = a\sqrt{1-c_k^2}.       (13)

(i)  Quadratic identity (3).

  = (2/3)^3,                      (17')

Either way,

Equality holds precisely when s_1=s_2=s_3=1/3, i.e. |c_1|=|c_2|=|c_3|=1/\sqrt{3},
which by (13) is equivalent to |\zeta _1|=|\zeta _2|=|\zeta _3|.  This is (7).

(iii)  Reconstruction of the cube from prescribed complex projections.  
[Identical to the previous solution and reproduced here for completeness.]

Let z_1,z_2,z_3 satisfy (8) and define
 X := (Re z_1,Re z_2,Re z_3), Y := (Im z_1,Im z_2,Im z_3),
 L := \|X\| = \|Y\| (>0).               (18)

From (8) we have X\cdot Y = 0.  Put
 Z := (1/L) X\times Y.                  (19)

Since X\bot Y and |X\times Y| = L^2, the 3 \times  3 matrix
 R := 1/L \cdot 
    Re z_1 Re z_2 Re z_3 
    Im z_1 Im z_2 Im z_3

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Solution to Part (B)
